title: GDPR Consent Management Audit Trail

compliance.assurance_rationale: This UC supplies two of the three evidentiary primitives Art.7 demands of a controller. (a) For Art.7(1) demonstration of consent: each grant event carries the IAB TCF v2.2 `consent_string` (which encodes the purposes, the Global Vendor List version, and the publisher purposes the data subject was shown) plus the CMP banner `ui_version` (which links to the legal text version that was on screen). The combination is replayable — a controller can show the regulator exactly what was put to a subject at grant time. (b) For Art.7(3) withdrawal-as-easy-as-grant: the per-purpose 7-day rolling withdrawal-to-grant ratio quantifies parity; on a stable population of >1000 grants per purpose, a ratio under 5% is a statistically meaningful red flag of an asymmetric flow (the metric is the same shape EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 §113–115 use to evaluate parity). The UC does NOT prove Art.7(2) intelligibility (which needs human review of the banner text) or Art.7(4) freely-given (which needs banner-display-to-grant timing analysis to detect cookie-wall and accept-to-read patterns; deferred to UC-22.1.6). Hence partial, not full.
compliance.controlObjective: Per-purpose evidence that every grant carries a parseable TCF v2.2 consent_string and ui_version (Art.7(1) demonstration), and that the rolling withdrawal-to-grant ratio sits inside the privacy-office-signed band evidencing withdrawal-as-easy-as-grant parity (Art.7(3)).
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Per-purpose row in audit_evidence (7y retention, privacy_office ACL): purpose_id, grants, withdrawals, ratio, grants_missing_consent_string, art7_1_evidence_gap, art7_3_parity_gap. Plus raw CMP records (subject_id_hash, action, purpose_id, tcf_version, consent_string, ui_version, geo, ts_grant/ts_withdraw) in the consent index. Weekly CSV digest to ServiceNow GRC for DPIA review.

dataSources: Two complementary sources are joined. (1) **CMP webhook events via HEC** at `index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event"` — structured JSON emitted by an IAB TCF v2.2-compliant Consent Management Platform (tested on OneTrust, TrustArc, Didomi, Cookiebot, Iubenda) carrying `subject_id` (pseudonymous hash), `action` (`grant` | `withdraw` | `update`), `purpose_id` (IAB TCF v2.2 purpose 1–11), `purpose_name`, `legal_basis`, `consent_string` (TCF v2.2 base64url), `tcf_version`, `ui_version` (banner build code), `geo`, `gdpr_applies`, `ts`. (2) **Apache and nginx access logs** at `index=web sourcetype="access_combined"` (and `sourcetype="nginx:access"` if applicable) — every HTTP-level interaction with the consent UI: GETs of the banner URI, POSTs to the consent and withdrawal endpoints. The CMP ledger is authoritative for payload; the web logs are the corroborating fingerprint that survives a CMP webhook outage.
app: `Splunk_TA_apache` ([Splunkbase 3186](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3186)), Splunk-supported, deployed to Universal Forwarders on the web tier and to every Search Head consuming the search. Splunk **HTTP Event Collector** (HEC) — platform capability of Splunk Enterprise 9.x and Splunk Cloud, configured via Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector with a dedicated `cmp_tcf_webhook` token scoped to `index=consent` and sourcetype `cmp:tcf:event`. nginx (where present) ingested either via Universal Forwarder file inputs with a private `props.conf` for `nginx:access` OR via the community `TA-nginx` ([Splunkbase 3258](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3258); community-supported, NOT Splunk-supported — fold its props.conf into a private TA if compliance requires Splunk-supported add-ons only).
spl:
(index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event" earliest=-7d@d)
OR (index=web sourcetype="access_combined" earliest=-7d@d (uri="/cmp/banner" OR uri="/api/consent" OR uri="/cmp/withdraw"))
| eval action = case(
    sourcetype=="cmp:tcf:event", action,
    method=="POST" AND like(uri,"/api/consent%"), "grant",
    method=="POST" AND like(uri,"/cmp/withdraw%"), "withdraw",
    1=1, "view"
  )
| where action IN ("grant","withdraw")
| where coalesce(gdpr_applies,"true")=="true"
| eval purpose_id = coalesce(purpose_id, "unspecified")
| eval consent_string_ok = case(
    action=="withdraw", 1,
    action=="grant" AND match(coalesce(consent_string,""), "^C[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}$") AND tcf_version=="2.2", 1,
    1=1, 0
  )
| eval ui_version_ok = if(isnotnull(ui_version) AND ui_version!="", 1, 0)
| stats count(eval(action=="grant")) as grants
        count(eval(action=="withdraw")) as withdrawals
        sum(eval(if(action=="grant" AND consent_string_ok==0, 1, 0))) as grants_missing_consent_string
        sum(eval(if(action=="grant" AND ui_version_ok==0, 1, 0))) as grants_missing_ui_version
        by purpose_id
| eval withdrawal_to_grant_ratio = round(withdrawals / if(grants>0, grants, 1), 4)
| eval art7_1_evidence_gap = if(grants_missing_consent_string>0 OR grants_missing_ui_version>0, "yes", "no")
| eval art7_3_parity_gap = if(grants>1000 AND withdrawal_to_grant_ratio<0.05, "yes", "no")
| where art7_1_evidence_gap="yes" OR art7_3_parity_gap="yes"
| sort - grants
description: Detects gaps in the GDPR Article 7 evidence chain by joining web-server consent endpoint hits with structured Consent Management Platform (CMP) events and flagging two failure modes per IAB TCF v2.2 purpose: (a) grant events recorded without a parseable TCF consent_string or `ui_version` — the controller cannot demonstrate to a regulator what terms were shown to the data subject (Art.7(1)); and (b) populations with more than 1000 grants per purpose where the rolling 7-day withdrawal-to-grant ratio falls below 5%, indicating that the withdrawal flow is materially harder than the grant flow (Art.7(3) parity gap).
value: Article 7 is the operational hinge of every EU consumer-facing app: a single failed regulator demonstration of consent under Art.7(1), or a finding that withdrawal is harder than grant under Art.7(3), is the path most often cited in supervisory-authority enforcement letters and class-action statements of claim. CNIL's January 2023 TikTok cookie-consent decision (deliberation SAN-2022-026, EUR 5m) is the canonical case study — it turned not on a missing CMP but on the CMP's withdrawal flow being demonstrably harder than the grant flow under inspection. This UC turns the CMP's own ledger into a Splunk-side audit trail the controller can replay without depending on the CMP vendor's user interface, with a 7-year-retention restricted index ready for regulator inspection on demand.
implementation: Stand up a dedicated `consent` index, mint a HEC token scoped to it, point the CMP webhook (OneTrust / TrustArc / Didomi / Cookiebot / Iubenda) at the HEC endpoint with the TCF v2.2 payload enabled, and deploy `Splunk_TA_apache` to the web tier so consent endpoint hits land in `index=web`. Restrict both indexes to a `privacy_office` role using `srchIndexesAllowed` and a purpose-scoped `srchFilter`. Schedule the search Monday 07:00 with throttling per `purpose_id` for 14 days. Export the per-purpose ratio table and the per-event consent_string evidence to ServiceNow GRC for the quarterly DPIA review.
visualization: (1) Single-value tile pair: `purpose_id` count with `art7_1_evidence_gap=yes` (last 7d, red threshold ≥1) and `purpose_id` count with `art7_3_parity_gap=yes` (last 7d, red threshold ≥1). (2) Stacked bar of grants vs withdrawals split by `purpose_id`, time-picker default 30 days, with the per-purpose name joined from a `consent_purposes.csv` lookup. (3) Sortable table — `purpose_id`, `purpose_name`, `grants`, `withdrawals`, `withdrawal_to_grant_ratio`, `grants_missing_consent_string`, `grants_missing_ui_version`, `art7_1_evidence_gap`, `art7_3_parity_gap`; row click drilldowns to the per-event raw search. (4) Timechart (line) of `withdrawal_to_grant_ratio` per `purpose_id` over 90 days — the trace the quarterly DPIA reviewer reads. (5) Drift tile: count of POSTs to `/api/consent` from `access_combined` versus count of `action=grant` events from the CMP, last 7d, drift % — red threshold ≥5% — pointed at CMP webhook availability rather than at Art.7 itself.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Apache Web Server (Splunkbase 3186)
Splunk HTTP Event Collector — Use the HTTP Event Collector
GDPR Article 7 — Conditions for consent (regulator text)
EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 on consent under Regulation 2016/679
IAB Europe Transparency & Consent Framework v2.2 (TCF v2.2 specification)
CNIL deliberation SAN-2022-026 — TikTok €5m cookie-consent fine (January 2023)

knownFalsePositives: This UC reads the CMP's own ledger and the website's URL surface, so the most common false-positives come from gaps between the two, from CMP version churn, and from territorial-scope handling. (a) **TCF version mismatch** — the CMP upgrades from TCF v2.0 to v2.2 mid-window and the regex `^C[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}$` plus the `tcf_version=='2.2'` clause flags the in-flight v2.0 events as `art7_1_evidence_gap=yes` even though they were perfectly valid at grant time; pin the regex to the version the CMP was running when the event was minted (use a small lookup keyed on `_time` or on a CMP release tag), not to the current version. (b) **Subject ID rotation on cookie clear** — when a data subject clears cookies the CMP issues a new pseudonymous `subject_id`; the prior grant events become orphaned and the same person appears as multiple subjects; the per-purpose ratio metric is unaffected because it counts events not subjects, but the per-subject withdrawal trace dashboard shows phantom subjects and should be filtered with the CMP's `subject_id_predecessor` field where available. (c) **Geo-conditional consent flows** — users outside the GDPR territorial scope (`gdpr_applies=false`) usually never see the banner and produce no grant events; if your CMP nevertheless logs a synthetic grant for them, the per-purpose grant count looks abnormally high and the withdrawal ratio looks abnormally low, mimicking an Art.7(3) parity gap; the SPL filters with `coalesce(gdpr_applies,'true')=='true'` precisely so non-EU traffic is excluded — sanity-check this filter is doing what you expect by comparing the count to your CMP's own EU-only audit log. (d) **CMP webhook outage with backfill** — the CMP buffers events for hours or days during a webhook outage and then floods them in once the webhook recovers, briefly inflating the grant count and dropping the withdrawal ratio in the recovery window; correlate every alert with `index=_internal source=*splunkd_access.log* hec` plus the CMP vendor's own outage log before filing a privacy ticket. (e) **Re-consent campaigns** — when terms of service or the privacy notice changes, every existing user must re-grant under Art.7, producing a spike in `action=grant` events that lasts 14–30 days; whitelist the campaign window via a `consent_campaigns.csv` lookup so the spike is not misread as either organic user growth or a withdrawal-side dark pattern. (f) **A/B test of the consent banner** — `ui_version` churns rapidly during a banner experiment; `grants_missing_ui_version` may stay zero but the `ui_version` distribution per `purpose_id` becomes spiky and the privacy office's `consent_ui_versions.csv` lookup falls behind. Pause the alert for the experiment window OR pre-load every experiment build into the lookup before launch.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites • `Splunk_TA_apache` ([Splunkbase 3186](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3186)) ≥3.0 installed on Universal Forwarders for the public web tier and on every Search Head consuming the search. • Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC) enabled — Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector → Global Settings → All Tokens = Enabled, Enable SSL = Enabled, HTTP Port = 8088. TLS terminating on a publicly-trusted certificate chain (most CMP vendors will reject a HEC endpoint fronted by an internal CA without manual import on their side). • A Consent Management Platform that emits IAB TCF v2.2-compliant webhook events. Tested on OneTrust ≥6.36, TrustArc Cookie Consent Manager ≥2024.07, Didomi Web SDK ≥1.85, Cookiebot ≥4.x, Iubenda Cookie Solution. Older CMPs that still emit TCF v2.0 require an upstream translator before HEC because v2.0 strings will be rejected by the regex+tcf_version gate in Step 2. • Two indexes. `consent` for the structured CMP events — 7-year retention to align with the longest EU member-state limitation period for civil claims grounded in unlawful processing; ACL restricted to a `privacy_office` role. Configure in `indexes.conf`: ```ini [consent] homePath = $SPLUNK_DB/consent/db coldPath = $SPLUNK_DB/consent/colddb thawedPath = $SPLUNK_DB/consent/thaweddb frozenTimePeriodInSecs = 220752000 maxTotalDataSizeMB = 500000 ``` `web` is your existing Apache/nginx index with shorter retention; this UC does not require extending it because the consent index holds the authoritative ledger. • Splunk role `privacy_office` with `srchIndexesAllowed = consent;web` and a purpose-scoped `srchFilter` that limits to consent endpoint URIs and to GDPR-in-scope CMP events: ```ini [role_privacy_office] srchIndexesAllowed = consent;web srchFilter = ((index=consent gdpr_applies=true) OR (index=web (uri="/cmp/banner" OR uri="/api/consent" OR uri="/cmp/withdraw"))) ``` The `srchFilter` is what enforces purpose-scoped access; without it, anyone with `consent` access can read every CMP event in every region. • A `consent_purposes.csv` lookup mapping `purpose_id` → `purpose_name`, `legal_basis`, `data_categories`, `retention_days`, owned by the privacy office and version-controlled in git with peer review on each change. • A `consent_ui_versions.csv` lookup mapping `ui_version` → `legal_text_url`, `effective_from`, `effective_to`. Drives the validation step and the audit replay flow. • Knowledge of the EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 §113–115 test for parity (operative reference for the Art.7(3) threshold tuning). Step 1 — Configure data collection (1) Create the HEC token. Splunk Web → Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector → New Token. Name `cmp_tcf_webhook`. Allowed source-types: `cmp:tcf:event`. Default index: `consent`. Disable indexer acknowledgment ONLY if the CMP vendor cannot send the `X-Splunk-Request-Channel` header (most modern CMPs can — leave acknowledgment enabled for at-least-once semantics). Copy the token UUID — you will paste it into the CMP webhook config in step (2). Never log the token in the CMP debug interface; rotate it on the same cadence as your other ingest credentials. (2) Configure the CMP webhook. The exact UI differs per vendor; common shape: — OneTrust: Admin Console → Integrations → Webhooks → Add → Endpoint = `https://:8088/services/collector/event`, Authorization = `Splunk `, Event filter = `consent.granted`, `consent.withdrawn`, `consent.updated`. Enable Admin Console → Cookie Consent → Settings → Audit Log → Include TCF Consent String. Without that toggle, `consent_string` is absent on every grant event. — TrustArc: Cookie Consent Manager → Integrations → Webhook URL → paste the HEC URL, set the `Authorization: Splunk ` header, enable the Consent Audit Log option in the same panel, enable the extended audit format toggle. — Didomi: Console → Webhooks → New webhook → URL = HEC, Header `Authorization: Splunk `, Event types = `consent.given`, `consent.changed`, `consent.withdrawn`. — Cookiebot: Manager → Integrations → External logging → Splunk HEC. — Iubenda: Privacy Controls and Cookie Solution → Integrations → Webhook → paste HEC URL with `Authorization` header. Always POST to `/services/collector/event` (which accepts JSON arrays). Do NOT use `/services/collector/raw`; raw endpoints concatenate fields and break the JSON-mode field extractions the SPL relies on. (3) Verify HEC ingestion. Trigger a test event via the CMP UI (each vendor exposes a "send test" button) and run `index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event" earliest=-15m | head 5 | table _time action purpose_id tcf_version consent_string ui_version subject_id`. Five rows with readable values, not raw text dumps — that means the JSON parser works. If only one row appears for a multi-event webhook payload, the CMP is sending a JSON array per webhook and HEC is treating the whole array as one event; switch the CMP to per-event POSTs OR add `INDEXED_EXTRACTIONS = json` and `KV_MODE = none` to a `props.conf` for the `cmp:tcf:event` sourcetype so HEC unrolls the array. (4) Deploy `Splunk_TA_apache` to the web tier. On the Deployment Server, push the TA to a serverclass mapping all front-end web hosts. Edit `etc/deployment-apps/Splunk_TA_apache/local/inputs.conf` to point at the actual log path: ```ini [monitor:///var/log/apache2/access*.log] disabled = 0 sourcetype = access_combined index = web ``` For nginx hosts, deploy a parallel TA bundle. Either ingest with the community `TA-nginx` ([Splunkbase 3258](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/3258); community-supported, NOT Splunk-supported) or bundle a private TA with a `props.conf` for `nginx:access`: ```ini [monitor:///var/log/nginx/access*.log] disabled = 0 sourcetype = nginx:access index = web ``` If compliance requires Splunk-supported add-ons only, fold the nginx props.conf into your private TA rather than depending on the community build. (5) Confirm the Apache events are landing: `index=web sourcetype="access_combined" uri="/cmp/banner" earliest=-15m | stats count`. A non-zero count after a synthetic page view = success. Step 2 — Create the search and alert Run this SPL after at least 24 hours of CMP events have accumulated: ```spl (index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event" earliest=-7d@d) OR (index=web sourcetype="access_combined" earliest=-7d@d (uri="/cmp/banner" OR uri="/api/consent" OR uri="/cmp/withdraw")) | eval action = case( sourcetype=="cmp:tcf:event", action, method=="POST" AND like(uri,"/api/consent%"), "grant", method=="POST" AND like(uri,"/cmp/withdraw%"), "withdraw", 1=1, "view" ) | where action IN ("grant","withdraw") | where coalesce(gdpr_applies,"true")=="true" | eval purpose_id = coalesce(purpose_id, "unspecified") | eval consent_string_ok = case( action=="withdraw", 1, action=="grant" AND match(coalesce(consent_string,""), "^C[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}$") AND tcf_version=="2.2", 1, 1=1, 0 ) | eval ui_version_ok = if(isnotnull(ui_version) AND ui_version!="", 1, 0) | stats count(eval(action=="grant")) as grants count(eval(action=="withdraw")) as withdrawals sum(eval(if(action=="grant" AND consent_string_ok==0, 1, 0))) as grants_missing_consent_string sum(eval(if(action=="grant" AND ui_version_ok==0, 1, 0))) as grants_missing_ui_version by purpose_id | eval withdrawal_to_grant_ratio = round(withdrawals / if(grants>0, grants, 1), 4) | eval art7_1_evidence_gap = if(grants_missing_consent_string>0 OR grants_missing_ui_version>0, "yes", "no") | eval art7_3_parity_gap = if(grants>1000 AND withdrawal_to_grant_ratio<0.05, "yes", "no") | where art7_1_evidence_gap="yes" OR art7_3_parity_gap="yes" | sort - grants ``` Why union the CMP ledger with Apache `access_combined`? The CMP is the authoritative source — `consent_string`, `purpose_id`, `tcf_version` only exist there — but the CMP can drop events during a webhook outage. The Apache events record the raw HTTP fingerprint of every consent UI interaction whether the CMP sent the webhook or not. Joining the two lets the operator detect the case where the CMP grant count differs materially from the count of POSTs to `/api/consent` (a sign that the webhook is dropping events the CMP UI's own audit log will hide). When the two counts disagree by more than 5%, escalate as a CMP availability incident BEFORE running the parity check; otherwise the parity check fires on stale data. Why pin the `consent_string` regex to TCF v2.2? IAB TCF v2.2 was released 2023-05 and is currently the mandatory revision for IAB member organisations operating in the EU; v2.0 strings are still parseable by older clients but legally are no longer evidence of the current Global Vendor List version. v1.x strings are no longer evidence at all. The regex `^C[A-Za-z0-9_-]{20,}$` matches the base64url body with the leading literal `C` that all TCF strings begin with; combined with `tcf_version=="2.2"` it rejects non-TCF events (a CMP misconfigured to emit an internal proprietary string) and rejects v1.x/v2.0 events. Why threshold the parity check at >1000 grants per purpose? Below 1000 grants per 7-day window the ratio is dominated by random noise (a single withdrawal can flip a 50-grant population from 0% to 2%); above 1000 the ratio stabilises and a sub-5% withdrawal rate becomes statistically meaningful as evidence of an Art.7(3) parity gap. Tune both thresholds in the second-running phase of the rollout; the privacy office should sign off on the band per purpose using a `consent_purpose_thresholds.csv` lookup once the seven-day distribution per purpose is understood. Schedule as Alert: cron `0 7 * * 1` (Monday 07:00 — runs after weekend events are fully indexed), time range `-7d@d to now`, trigger on "Number of results > 0". Throttle by `purpose_id` for 14 days so a stable Art.7(3) parity gap does not page the DPO every Monday until remediation lands. Notify the DPO and the privacy engineering distribution list, NOT the on-call SRE — this is a regulatory and legal signal, not an outage. Step 3 — Validate (a) Match a Splunk grant event back to the CMP UI's own audit log. Pick a known synthetic test: in a private browser session with the CMP's preview-mode flag on, click "Allow All". Note the timestamp. In Splunk: `index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event" subject_id= action=grant earliest=-15m | head 1 | table _time action purpose_id consent_string ui_version`. Compare the `consent_string` against the CMP's audit-log row for the same `subject_id`. They must match byte-for-byte. A difference indicates HEC payload truncation (HEC default `maxEventSize` is 65535 bytes — TCF v2.2 strings with extended publisher purposes can approach this) or a CMP webhook config that strips fields (some vendors require an explicit "include consent_string" toggle as called out in Step 1). (b) Decode the `consent_string` end-to-end. The IAB TCF v2.2 consent string is base64url-encoded TCF Core String (purposes 1–11) plus optional Disclosed Vendors and Publisher TC. Decode externally — the IAB maintains a CMP validator at `https://iabtechlab.com/cmp-validator/` — and confirm the purpose flags match the user's clicks. If the SPL says `consent_string_ok=1` but the decoded purposes are all zero, the user actually rejected everything and the CMP is incorrectly emitting `action=grant`. That is a Step 4 incident: open a P2 with the CMP vendor, AND redact the affected events from the audit pack until the source is fixed. (c) Walk back from a per-purpose row to a representative event: `index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event" purpose_id=

action=grant earliest=-7d@d | head 5 | table _time subject_id consent_string ui_version geo gdpr_applies`. Spot-check that `ui_version` matches a row in `consent_ui_versions.csv`; a `ui_version` you do not recognise means a banner build is in production that the privacy office did not sign off on — open a UI change-control ticket immediately. (d) Confirm the role enforces the index ACL: `| rest splunk_server=local /servicesNS/-/-/authorization/roles | search title=privacy_office | table title srchIndexesAllowed srchFilter`. The list must include `consent` and `web`; the `srchFilter` must be present and non-empty. Without `srchFilter`, the role can read all CMP events in all regions, which is over-broad for a privacy-engineering role and may itself create a data-protection-by-design finding under Art.25. (e) Confirm CIM compliance is NOT required: this UC operates on custom JSON; no CIM data model normalises consent events as of CIM 6.0 and no Splunk acceleration applies. Do not attempt to coerce `cmp:tcf:event` into the Web data model — the field semantics do not align and the resulting tstats will be silently wrong. Step 4 — Operationalize Owner: Data Protection Officer (DPO). Co-owners: Privacy Engineering team lead (CMP and HEC infrastructure), Web Platform team lead (Apache/nginx), legal counsel (Art.7 interpretation and EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 application). Dashboard layout (recommended, name "GDPR Art.7 Consent Audit Trail"): • Row 1 — Single-value tile pair: "purpose_ids with art7_1 evidence gap (last 7d)" and "purpose_ids with art7_3 parity gap (last 7d)". Red threshold ≥1 on either tile. • Row 2 — Stacked bar of grants vs withdrawals split by `purpose_id`, last 30 days, with `purpose_name` joined from `consent_purposes.csv`. • Row 3 — Sortable per-purpose table (the work queue): `purpose_id`, `purpose_name`, `grants`, `withdrawals`, `withdrawal_to_grant_ratio`, `grants_missing_consent_string`, `grants_missing_ui_version`, `art7_1_evidence_gap`, `art7_3_parity_gap`. Drilldown opens the per-event raw search filtered to that `purpose_id`. • Row 4 — Timechart (line) of `withdrawal_to_grant_ratio` per `purpose_id` over 90 days. The trace the quarterly DPIA reviewer reads. • Row 5 — Drift tile: count of POSTs to `/api/consent` in `access_combined` versus count of `action=grant` events from the CMP, last 7d, drift %. Red threshold ≥5%, pointed at CMP webhook availability rather than at Art.7 itself. Retention and ACL: • `consent` index — 7-year retention via `frozenTimePeriodInSecs = 220752000`. ACL: `srchIndexesAllowed` and `srchFilter` from Prerequisites enforce role-scoped, purpose-scoped access. Encryption at rest is provided by Splunk Cloud or, on Splunk Enterprise, by the underlying disk subsystem (LUKS, dm-crypt, or platform KMS-managed volumes). FIPS-mode operation is required where the controller's ROPA classifies consent records as restricted. • `web` index — typically already present with a shorter retention; this UC does not require extending it because the `consent` index holds the authoritative Art.7(1) demonstration evidence. Evidence export: • Weekly: a scheduled saved search emails the per-purpose CSV to the ServiceNow GRC inbox. Include the SPL itself, the `_time` window, and a hash of the result set in the email body so the auditor can reproduce and challenge the report. • Quarterly: the DPIA reviewer cycle pulls the 90-day per-purpose ratio history (Row 4 of the dashboard) and the per-purpose table (Row 3); archived to the GRC artefact store. If the optional `evidenceSigning.method = rfc3161-tsa` is enabled, the export is RFC 3161 timestamped against a trusted TSA so the artefact's integrity is provable to the regulator. • Ad hoc — regulator request or Art.15 SAR: extract the raw CMP events for the requested `subject_id` from the consent index along with the per-event `consent_string`. Decode externally and present alongside the relevant `ui_version` legal text from `consent_ui_versions.csv`. This package is what "demonstrate consent" under Art.7(1) means in practice. Runbook (owner: DPO): 1. `art7_1_evidence_gap=yes` → open the per-event raw search for the offending `purpose_id`; identify whether the missing field is `consent_string` (likely a CMP misconfiguration or v1.x/v2.0 event) or `ui_version` (likely a banner build that bypassed the CI gate). Engage the Web Platform team to fix at source within 48 hours; in parallel, document the gap in the next DPIA reviewer cycle. Do NOT silently retire the affected events from the audit pack — annotate them as "unverifiable" so the regulator sees the gap was identified and remediated. 2. `art7_3_parity_gap=yes` → compare the user-facing flow: clicks-to-grant versus clicks-to-withdraw, button colour parity, banner scroll position, dark-pattern indicators. EDPB Guidelines 05/2020 §113–115 are the operative parity test. If the flow is genuinely asymmetric, file a UI change request with a 14-day SLA; if it is symmetric on inspection but the metric still fires, increase the >1000 grants threshold or extend the 7-day window in `consent_purpose_thresholds.csv`. 3. Apache vs CMP drift >5% → CMP webhook outage or HEC token throttling. Open a P2 with the CMP vendor; in parallel, spool the missed period using the CMP's own audit log export and replay-load to the consent index with `_time` preserved (HEC accepts an explicit `time` field on the JSON event). Step 5 — Troubleshooting • **Zero `cmp:tcf:event` events after CMP webhook configured** — HEC token rejected. Tail `index=_internal source=*splunkd_access.log* sourcetype=splunkd_access | search hec OR collector earliest=-15m | head 20`. A 401 means the token is wrong or disabled; a 403 means the token's `indexes` setting does not include `consent`; a 404 means the CMP is hitting `/services/collector` instead of `/services/collector/event`. Fix at the CMP webhook config; never paste the token into the CMP's debug log, it is a credential. • **`consent_string` is empty on every grant event** — the CMP is sending the webhook but its payload schema does not include the consent string by default. OneTrust requires Admin Console → Cookie Consent → Settings → Audit Log → Include TCF Consent String = Enabled; TrustArc requires the extended audit format toggle; Didomi exposes it under the webhook payload settings. If the CMP does not expose the `consent_string` at all (some legacy CMPs), this UC cannot satisfy Art.7(1) demonstration of consent. Replace the CMP, OR supplement with a Splunk-side `consent_string` reconstruction from per-purpose flags (best-effort; not regulator-grade evidence and must be flagged as such in the audit pack). • **`tcf_version` is `2.0` not `2.2`** — the CMP is still on the older TCF revision. Most major vendors are TCF v2.2-compliant since 2023-11. Until the CMP is upgraded, change the SPL regex to accept both — replace the `tcf_version=="2.2"` clause with `tcf_version IN ("2.0","2.1","2.2")` — but note that v2.0 events reference a Global Vendor List version no longer current; flag the affected time window in the audit pack so the regulator knows which GVL was in force. • **`art7_3_parity_gap` fires every week on the same `purpose_id`, but the privacy office insists the flow is parity-compliant** — the >1000 grants threshold is too high or the 5% withdrawal-ratio band is too tight for that purpose. Run `index=consent sourcetype="cmp:tcf:event" action=grant earliest=-90d@d | stats count by purpose_id | sort - count` and `... | stats avg(withdrawal_to_grant_ratio) p95(withdrawal_to_grant_ratio) by purpose_id` over 90 days, then tune per-purpose using a `consent_purpose_thresholds.csv` lookup with columns `min_grants_for_parity_check` and `max_acceptable_ratio`. Replace the SPL constants with `lookup` values. • **`subject_id` looks like 32 hex on some events and like base64 on others** — your CMP rotated the `subject_id` format mid-window. Harmless for the per-purpose ratio metric but breaks subject-level traces and Art.15 SAR fulfilment. Add a normaliser: `| eval subject_id_norm = if(match(subject_id,"^[a-f0-9]{32}$"), subject_id, lower(sha256(subject_id)))`. Document the format change in the privacy office's audit log so an Art.15 reviewer knows which format applied in which window. • **Apache POST count to `/api/consent` is consistently 2–3× higher than CMP grant count** — the front-end is double-firing the consent endpoint, typical when both an inline JS handler and a CMP `onConsent` callback POST. The CMP's de-duplication logic produces one webhook per logical grant, so the metric is correct; ignore the Apache drift OR add an `X-Consent-Source: primary` header to the CMP's POST and filter the Apache search by that header to deduplicate. • **Same `purpose_id` appears with two different `purpose_name` values across the 7-day window** — a `consent_purposes.csv` change happened during the window. Consult the privacy office's lookup version-control history; the audit pack must record both names with their `effective_from` dates so an auditor can reconstruct what the data subject was shown. • **Search runs longer than 5 minutes against a high-traffic CMP** — the union of consent and web is fan-out-heavy on a busy estate. Switch to a precomputed approach: a nightly saved search builds `consent_audit_daily_rollup` summary-index events with `purpose_id, day, grants, withdrawals, grants_missing_consent_string`; the alert SPL becomes `index=summary source=consent_audit_daily_rollup earliest=-7d | stats sum(grants) sum(withdrawals) ... by purpose_id`. The summary-index variant also enables the 90-day ratio trending on Row 4 of the dashboard with sub-second response. • **`gdpr_applies` is empty on legacy CMP events** — older CMP webhook payloads omit `gdpr_applies` entirely and the SPL's `coalesce(gdpr_applies,"true")` defaults to `"true"`, pulling non-EU traffic into the audit. Either upgrade the CMP to a payload schema that includes the field, OR change the default to `"false"` and explicitly mark the events your CMP knows are GDPR-in-scope using the CMP's `geo` field plus an EU member-state allow-list lookup.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an unbroken audit trail of every time someone clicks 'allow' or 'withdraw consent' on the cookie or privacy banner — what they agreed to, what version of the wording they were shown, and when they changed their mind. If the 'withdraw' button stops working as easily as the 'allow' button, the trail tells us before the regulator does.
